name: Exterior & Signage Refresh
purpose: Ensures the cafe's exterior and signage create a compelling first impression that draws passers-by inside and communicates the brand's identity, quality, and welcome from the street. This block treats the exterior as the cafe's most powerful silent salesperson — working 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, whether the cafe is open or not.

detailed_objectives: Audit the exterior appearance from a first-time visitor's perspective and identify issues that deter entry
Ensure primary signage (fascia, projecting sign) is clear, well-maintained, and reflective of the brand identity
Optimise window displays and A-frame boards to communicate the offer and create desire
Maintain exterior cleanliness and upkeep so the outside reflects the quality of the inside
Maximise kerb appeal to convert walk-past traffic into walk-in customers
secondary_objectives: Ensure the cafe is visible and identifiable from multiple approach directions
Create an inviting entrance that feels welcoming rather than intimidating to new customers
Comply with local planning regulations regarding signage, A-frames, and outdoor displays
Extend brand visibility through outdoor elements: planters, awnings, lighting, and furniture

why_this_matters: A potential customer walking past a cafe makes a decision about whether to enter within 3-7 seconds. In that window, the exterior must communicate three things: what the cafe is, what quality to expect, and that they are welcome. Poor signage, a dirty facade, opaque windows, or an uninviting entrance can cost a cafe dozens of potential customers every day without the owner ever knowing. The cumulative revenue impact is enormous — if even 5 additional walk-past customers per day are converted into visitors spending an average of 6 pounds, that is over 10,000 pounds in additional annual revenue. Exterior presentation is also a hygiene factor for online research. When a customer finds the cafe on Google Maps and clicks 'Street View', the exterior they see either confirms their intention to visit or causes doubt. Instagram-tagged photos of the exterior create permanent impressions. Delivery drivers arriving to collect orders form opinions about the business from the outside. The exterior is the first and last impression a customer has of the cafe, and it shapes expectations for everything inside. Many cafe owners focus intensely on interior design and product quality but neglect the exterior because they do not see it from the street — they enter through the back door or are already inside by the time they start their day. A deliberate, regular exterior audit corrects this blind spot. In the UK, planning regulations govern what signage a cafe can display, and breaching these rules can result in enforcement action. Understanding these regulations before investing in new signage saves money and avoidance of potential fines.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Walk the approach to the cafe from every direction a customer might come — from the left, right, and across the street. Note: Can you see the cafe from 50 metres away? Is the name clearly readable from 20 metres? Do the windows show the interior or are they obscured by posters, blinds, or reflections? Is the entrance obvious — can a new customer immediately see where to go in? Is the facade clean, with no peeling paint, grimy windows, or overflowing bins? Take photos from each approach direction as documentation. Ask a friend who has never visited to do the same walk and give candid feedback. Week 2: Address the most impactful quick wins. Clean the exterior thoroughly: windows, frames, door handles, any outdoor furniture, and the pavement immediately outside (pressure wash if needed). If the signage is faded, damaged, or unclear, get a quote for replacement — but as an interim measure, ensure it is at least clean and lit. If the window display is cluttered with old posters, strip it back and create a simple, clean display showing the cafe's best offerings. Set up or refresh the A-frame board with a clear, enticing daily message — not a full menu, but a single compelling proposition: 'Freshly baked croissants every morning' or 'Specialty coffee from 7am'. Check that the entrance door is clean, the handle works smoothly, and there is nothing blocking or discouraging entry.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Commission a signage review. If the primary fascia sign is tired, outdated, or does not reflect the current brand identity (B140), plan for replacement. Check local planning regulations: most illuminated signs and projecting signs require planning permission or advertisement consent. Contact the local council's planning department before commissioning work. Budget 500-3,000 pounds for a quality fascia sign depending on size and illumination. Consider adding a projecting sign (hanging perpendicular to the building) for visibility along the street — these are particularly effective on busy high streets where fascia signs can be obscured by adjacent buildings. Develop a window display strategy: the window should be a curated, rotating display that communicates the cafe's current offer and personality. Change the display monthly or seasonally. Avoid cluttering windows with too many posters — less is more. If the cafe has an awning, ensure it is clean, in good repair, and branded if possible. Install or upgrade exterior lighting so the cafe looks inviting in the evening and during dark winter mornings. Create an exterior maintenance schedule: weekly window cleaning, monthly facade check, seasonal deep clean, and annual signage inspection.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The exterior should now be a consistent, brand-aligned, compelling invitation. Develop the exterior as part of the overall brand experience: planters with seasonal flowers, attractive outdoor furniture that matches the interior aesthetic, and lighting that creates atmosphere in the evening. If the cafe has outdoor seating, ensure it is well-maintained, clean, and inviting — outdoor seating that looks tired or uncomfortable deters rather than attracts. Create Instagram-worthy exterior elements: a distinctive painted wall, an attractive sign, or beautiful planters that customers photograph and share, generating organic marketing. Maintain a photographic record of the exterior across seasons and years, tracking improvements and identifying maintenance needs. Conduct a quarterly exterior audit using the same approach-walk methodology established in L1, ensuring standards do not slip. Coordinate exterior presentation with seasonal marketing: festive decorations in December, spring flowers in March, summer outdoor dining displays. These seasonal touches signal that the business is active, caring, and current.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Walk the approach from every direction and photograph the exterior from a customer's perspective
Identify the top 5 exterior issues that most impact first impressions
Deep clean the exterior: windows, facade, door, outdoor furniture, pavement
Strip back cluttered window displays to a clean, inviting arrangement
Set up or refresh the A-frame board with a single compelling daily proposition
Ensure the entrance is obvious, clean, and welcoming

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Commission a signage review and check local planning regulations
Plan and budget for fascia sign replacement or refurbishment if needed
Develop a monthly window display strategy
Install or upgrade exterior lighting for visibility during dark hours
Create an exterior maintenance schedule: weekly, monthly, seasonal tasks
Consider adding a projecting sign for improved street visibility

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Develop the exterior as part of the overall brand experience with planters, furniture, and lighting
Create Instagram-worthy exterior elements that generate organic customer photography
Conduct quarterly exterior audits using the approach-walk methodology
Coordinate exterior presentation with seasonal marketing and decorations
Maintain a photographic record of the exterior for tracking and planning
Review signage and exterior annually for brand alignment and physical condition

deliverables: Exterior Audit Report with photographs and recommended actions
Signage Specification and Planning Compliance Check
Window Display Strategy and Monthly Plan

tools.description: A structured checklist for auditing the cafe's exterior from every approach direction, covering signage visibility, facade condition, window display, entrance appeal, cleanliness, and overall first impression. Includes a scoring system and photo documentation prompts.
A monthly planning template for window displays specifying the theme, key items to feature, any printed materials needed, seasonal elements, and installation schedule.

A maintenance schedule covering weekly tasks (window cleaning, A-frame update, litter sweep), monthly tasks (facade check, planter maintenance), seasonal tasks (deep clean, decoration changes), and annual tasks (signage inspection, paint assessment).

A library of 30+ A-frame board messages organised by theme (daily specials, seasonal, humorous, informative) designed to be rotated daily. Each message is concise, compelling, and aligned with the cafe's brand voice.

failure_modes: The exterior becomes a blind spot because the owner enters through the back and never sees the cafe from the street
Signage investment is made without checking planning regulations, resulting in enforcement notices and wasted money
The A-frame board displays the same message for months, becoming invisible to regular passers-by
Window displays accumulate layers of old posters and notices, creating a cluttered, unprofessional look
kpis: Exterior audit completed quarterly with documented findings and actions
A-frame board message updated daily during trading hours
Windows cleaned weekly and window display refreshed at least monthly
Signage visible and readable from at least 20 metres on all approach routes
constraints: Landlord permission may be required for signage changes or facade alterations
Local planning regulations govern signage size, illumination, and placement
Budget for signage replacement can be significant (500-3,000 pounds) and may need to be phased

time_load.L1: 3 hours (exterior audit, deep clean, A-frame setup, window display refresh)
time_load.L2: 8-12 hours (signage review, planning check, maintenance schedule setup, lighting upgrade)

review_protocol: Daily A-frame and entrance check. Weekly window cleaning and exterior tidiness check. Monthly window display refresh. Quarterly full exterior audit. Annual signage condition and brand alignment review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block ensures the cafe's exterior and signage create a compelling first impression that converts passers-by into visitors. It covers signage, window displays, facade maintenance, A-frame boards, and the overall kerb appeal of the cafe from every approach direction.
implementation_risks: Signage projects can overrun on cost and timeline, particularly if planning permission is required
Exterior maintenance requires ongoing commitment — a single deep clean is not sufficient without a regular schedule
Overly ambitious exterior renovation can disrupt trading if not planned carefully
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate low walk-in conversion, poor exterior appearance, faded or damaged signage, or when the cafe is new and establishing its physical presence. Particularly impactful for cafes on busy streets where walk-past traffic is a primary acquisition channel. Links to B141 (New Customer Acquisition) and B146 (Front-of-House Cleanliness).
